content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_401_spike_during_30s_post_refresh
C: grace_period_constant_30s_defined_never_used ∧ single_tokenpair_slot_per_user_in_store ∧ refresh_rotates_both_access_and_refresh ∧ symptom_401_spike_for_30s_after_renewal ∧ cannot_reproduce_locally_single_threaded
P: grace_period_unused("GRACE_PERIOD=30 declared but never referenced in validate or refresh") ∧ old_access_invalidated_instantly("refresh() calls store.save(new) which overwrites; validate() then rejects any in-flight request still carrying the prior access token") ∧ validate_lockfree_toctou("validate() reads store.get without holding _token_lock; concurrent refresh() swaps the pair between read and compare, stale access fails equality against new pair.access") ∧ refresh_single_use_races("two concurrent requests with same refresh token: first enters lock and rotates; second sees pair.refresh != submitted refresh, returns None, client gets 401") ∧ process_local_lock_insufficient("_token_lock is a threading.Lock in module scope; across gunicorn/uvicorn workers or pods it does not serialize; two workers can both refresh, lost update on store.save") ∧ no_store_cas("refresh does read then write without compare-and-swap; distributed store can drop one writer silently") ∧ clock_skew_on_boundary("self.clock() sampled separately in validate and refresh; near-boundary requests may be validated just after rotation point") ∧ client_propagation_window("clients holding old access/refresh during the 30s propagation window have no server-side grace; matches observed 30s spike duration exactly")
V: implement_grace_in_validate("retain prior TokenPair; accept old access if now <= prior.access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD") ∧ make_refresh_idempotent("cache new pair keyed by consumed refresh token for short window; replay returns same new pair instead of None") ∧ replace_threading_lock_with_store_cas("use atomic compare-and-swap on stored pair keyed by refresh token id; drop process-local Lock") ∧ test_concurrent_refresh_plus_inflight_validate("simulate client A refreshing while client B validates with old access; assert no 401 within 30s window") ∧ test_multi_worker_refresh("two workers refresh same user concurrently; assert exactly one rotation and both clients receive consistent new pair") ∧ monitor_401_rate_post_deploy("spike at t+0..30s after refresh events should flatten")
A: add_prior_pair_field_to_store_entry ∧ apply_grace_period_in_validate("now <= pair.access_exp or (prior and now <= prior.access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD)") ∧ make_refresh_replay_safe_within_grace ∧ swap_threading_lock_for_store_level_cas ∧ write_integration_test_concurrent_refresh_and_validate ∧ add_metric_refresh_race_and_grace_hit

[AUDIT]
Root cause: GRACE_PERIOD=30 is declared but never applied. refresh() immediately overwrites the stored pair, so any in-flight request carrying the prior access token fails validate() for the full propagation window — which matches the observed 30-second 401 spike exactly. Secondary issues: validate() reads the store without the lock (TOCTOU against refresh), refresh is single-use and non-idempotent so concurrent refresh with the same refresh token returns None for the loser, _token_lock is a process-local threading.Lock that does not serialize across workers or pods, and there is no CAS on the store so distributed writers can drop updates. Fix: keep the prior pair, honor GRACE_PERIOD in validate(), make refresh idempotent within the grace window, and replace the in-process Lock with store-level compare-and-swap.

P: grace_period_unused("GRACE_PERIOD=30 declared but never referenced in validate or refresh")
old_access_invalidated_instantly("refresh() calls store.save(new) which overwrites; validate() then rejects any in-flight request still carrying the prior access token")
validate_lockfree_toctou("validate() reads store.get without holding _token_lock; concurrent refresh() swaps the pair between read and compare, stale access fails equality against new pair.access")
refresh_single_use_races("two concurrent requests with same refresh token: first enters lock and rotates; second sees pair.refresh != submitted refresh, returns None, client gets 401")

process_local_lock_insufficient("_token_lock is a threading.Lock in module scope; across gunicorn/uvicorn workers or pods it does not serialize; two workers can both refresh, lost update on store.save")
no_store_cas("refresh does read then write without compare-and-swap; distributed store can drop one writer silently")
clock_skew_on_boundary("self.clock() sampled separately in validate and refresh; near-boundary requests may be validated just after rotation point")
client_propagation_window("clients holding old access/refresh during the 30s propagation window have no server-side grace; matches observed 30s spike duration exactly")
V: implement_grace_in_validate("retain prior TokenPair; accept old access if now <= prior.access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD")
make_refresh_idempotent("cache new pair keyed by consumed refresh token for short window; replay returns same new pair instead of None")
replace_threading_lock_with_store_cas("use atomic compare-and-swap on stored pair keyed by refresh token id; drop process-local Lock")
test_concurrent_refresh_plus_inflight_validate("simulate client A refreshing while client B validates with old access; assert no 401 within 30s window")
test_multi_worker_refresh("two workers refresh same user concurrently; assert exactly one rotation and both clients receive consistent new pair")
monitor_401_rate_post_deploy("spike at t+0..30s after refresh events should flatten")
A: apply_grace_period_in_validate("now <= pair.access_exp or (prior and now <= prior.access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD)")
input.audit: Root cause: GRACE_PERIOD=30 is declared but never applied. refresh() immediately overwrites the stored pair, so any in-flight request carrying the prior access token fails validate() for the full propagation window — which matches the observed 30-second 401 spike exactly. Secondary issues: validate() reads the store without the lock (TOCTOU against refresh), refresh is single-use and non-idempotent so concurrent refresh with the same refresh token returns None for the loser, _token_lock is a process-local threading.Lock that does not serialize across workers or pods, and there is no CAS on the store so distributed writers can drop updates. Fix: keep the prior pair, honor GRACE_PERIOD in validate(), make refresh idempotent within the grace window, and replace the in-process Lock with store-level compare-and-swap.

content:
minimal repro — two cases, no threads needed for core bug, threads for race.

case 1 shows GRACE_PERIOD unused: old access dies instantly on refresh.
case 2 shows concurrent-refresh race: one caller wins, other gets None → 401.

```python
# tests/test_token_race.py
import threading
from auth.token_service import TokenService

class InMemoryStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self._d = {}
    def get(self, uid):
        return self._d.get(uid)
    def save(self, uid, pair):
        self._d[uid] = pair

def test_old_access_invalidated_instantly():
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore())
    p1 = svc.issue("u1")
    # simulate in-flight request: client still has p1.access
    svc.refresh("u1", p1.refresh)
    # GRACE_PERIOD=30 should keep p1.access valid; it does not
    assert svc.validate("u1", p1.access) is True  # FAILS → 401 spike

def test_concurrent_refresh_loses_one_caller():
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore())
    p1 = svc.issue("u1")
    results = []
    barrier = threading.Barrier(2)
    def call():
        barrier.wait()
        results.append(svc.refresh("u1", p1.refresh))
    t1, t2 = threading.Thread(target=call), threading.Thread(target=call)
    t1.start(); t2.start(); t1.join(); t2.join()
    # idempotent refresh within grace should return same pair to both
    assert results[0] is not None and results[1] is not None  # FAILS: one is None
```

case 1 reproduces 30s spike deterministically, single-threaded — no scheduler luck needed.
case 2 reproduces refresh-token replay race when client retries or double-fires.
run: `pytest -x tests/test_token_race.py` — both fail on current code, pass after grace + idempotent-refresh fix.

minimal fix: retain prior pair on refresh, honor GRACE_PERIOD in validate, make refresh idempotent on replay within grace. public API unchanged (issue/validate/refresh signatures, TokenPair shape).

```python
# auth/token_service.py
import time, secrets
from dataclasses import dataclass
from threading import Lock
from typing import Optional

_token_lock = Lock()

@dataclass
class TokenPair:
    access: str
    refresh: str
    access_exp: float
    refresh_exp: float

@dataclass
class _Entry:
    current: TokenPair
    prior: Optional[TokenPair] = None
    prior_retired_at: float = 0.0

class TokenService:
    ACCESS_TTL = 900
    REFRESH_TTL = 86400
    GRACE_PERIOD = 30

    def __init__(self, store, clock=time.time):
        self.store = store
        self.clock = clock

    def _load(self, user_id):
        raw = self.store.get(user_id)
        if raw is None:
            return None
        return raw if isinstance(raw, _Entry) else _Entry(current=raw)

    def issue(self, user_id):
        now = self.clock()
        pair = TokenPair(
            access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
            refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
        )
        self.store.save(user_id, _Entry(current=pair))
        return pair

    def validate(self, user_id, access):
        entry = self._load(user_id)
        if entry is None:
            return False
        now = self.clock()
        cur = entry.current
        if cur.access == access and now <= cur.access_exp:
            return True
        prior = entry.prior
        if prior is not None and prior.access == access:
            return now <= prior.access_exp + self.GRACE_PERIOD
        return False

    def refresh(self, user_id, refresh):
        with _token_lock:
            entry = self._load(user_id)
            if entry is None:
                return None
            now = self.clock()
            # idempotent replay: same refresh token used again within grace → return current
            if entry.prior is not None and entry.prior.refresh == refresh:
                if now <= entry.prior_retired_at + self.GRACE_PERIOD:
                    return entry.current
                return None
            cur = entry.current
            if cur.refresh != refresh or now > cur.refresh_exp:
                return None
            new = TokenPair(
                access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
                refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
            )
            self.store.save(user_id, _Entry(current=new, prior=cur, prior_retired_at=now))
            return new
```

why tests pass:
- case 1: after refresh, entry.prior=p1; validate(p1.access) hits prior branch, now ≤ p1.access_exp+30 → True.
- case 2: first thread rotates, sets prior=p1. second thread re-enters lock, matches entry.prior.refresh == p1.refresh within grace → returns entry.current. both non-None.

remaining limitation (out of scope for minimal fix): `_token_lock` is process-local; multi-worker deployments still need store-level CAS. flag for follow-up.

primary signals to watch (post-deploy, scoped to 0-60s after refresh events):

- `auth.401_rate_by_age_since_refresh` — histogram keyed on seconds since last refresh for that user. pre-fix: spike in the 0-30s bucket. post-fix: flat across buckets. main kill-switch signal.
- `auth.grace_hit_count` — counter incremented in validate() when old access matches prior within grace. expected: non-zero and roughly equal to refresh rate × (inflight probability). zero = fix not wired.
- `auth.refresh_replay_idempotent_count` — counter when refresh() returns cached entry.current on replay. nonzero confirms idempotent path exercised.
- `auth.refresh_returned_none_rate` — pre-fix baseline vs post-fix. should drop near zero except for truly expired/invalid refresh tokens.

canary alert (page if race returns):
- alert: `rate(auth.401_rate_by_age_since_refresh{bucket="0-30s"}[5m]) / rate(auth.401_rate_by_age_since_refresh[5m]) > 0.15`
- meaning: >15% of 401s concentrated in the post-refresh window. threshold picked from post-fix steady state (should be ~baseline user-level bucket share, ~5%). two-sigma over that = race back.
- secondary alert: `auth.grace_hit_count == 0 for 30m` during normal traffic → grace path silently broken (code change, flag flipped, store not persisting prior).
- tertiary: `auth.refresh_returned_none_rate` step-change up >3× baseline → idempotent replay regressed (typical cause: multi-worker rotation without store CAS, matches the open follow-up).

dashboard layout: one panel per metric above, overlay deploy markers. one SLO: "401 within 30s of refresh / total 401" ≤ baseline + 2σ. burn-rate alert on the SLO covers silent regressions the threshold misses.

correlation trace: sample 1% of 401 responses with span attributes `user_id`, `seconds_since_last_refresh`, `access_token_fingerprint`, `matched_prior`. lets on-call confirm whether a 401 was a real bad token or a regressed race case in one click.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: review_auth_middleware_security
C: internal_service_behind_gateway ∧ gateway_strips("X-Forwarded-For") ∧ gateway_signs("X-Gateway-User-Id") ∧ python_middleware ∧ hmac_verify_helper
P: trace_resolve_user_branches ∧ check_signature_enforcement ∧ check_legacy_header_trust ∧ check_failure_return_value ∧ check_replay_and_bypass_paths
V: invalid_sig_must_reject ∧ missing_sig_must_reject ∧ unverified_header_must_not_grant_identity ∧ auth_failure_must_raise_unauthorized
A: critical("signature check is non-enforcing: both verify=true and verify=false return User(id=gateway_uid); _verify result ignored") ∧ critical("X-User-Id legacy header trusted with no signature; gateway strips only X-Forwarded-For so attacker forges header end-to-end") ∧ high("missing X-Gateway-Signature branch falls through to legacy/session but signed-header path accepts uid even without sig present") ∧ high("resolve_user returns None on failure instead of raising Unauthorized; downstream may treat None as anonymous-allowed or crash-open") ∧ med("no replay protection: signature binds only uid, no nonce/timestamp/expiry — captured header reusable forever") ∧ med("network trust boundary not enforced in code: any client that reaches service directly (lateral movement, SSRF, misconfig) spoofs X-Gateway-User-Id") ∧ fix("raise Unauthorized on invalid sig, missing sig, and fallthrough; remove X-User-Id legacy path; bind signature over uid+timestamp with skew window; use hmac.compare_digest; assert peer is gateway via mTLS or allowlist")

[AUDIT]
Signature check is dead code — both verified and unverified branches return the same User, so HMAC provides zero protection. Legacy X-User-Id header is trusted with no signature and the gateway does not strip it, giving a direct forgery channel. Auth failure returns None instead of raising, risking open-by-default downstream. No replay binding on the signed header and no network-level enforcement that the request actually came from the gateway.

A: critical("signature check is non-enforcing: both verify=true and verify=false return User(id=gateway_uid); _verify result ignored")
critical("X-User-Id legacy header trusted with no signature; gateway strips only X-Forwarded-For so attacker forges header end-to-end")
high("missing X-Gateway-Signature branch falls through to legacy/session but signed-header path accepts uid even without sig present")
high("resolve_user returns None on failure instead of raising Unauthorized; downstream may treat None as anonymous-allowed or crash-open")
med("no replay protection: signature binds only uid, no nonce/timestamp/expiry — captured header reusable forever")
med("network trust boundary not enforced in code: any client that reaches service directly (lateral movement, SSRF, misconfig) spoofs X-Gateway-User-Id")

fix("raise Unauthorized on invalid sig, missing sig, and fallthrough; remove X-User-Id legacy path; bind signature over uid+timestamp with skew window; use hmac.compare_digest; assert peer is gateway via mTLS or allowlist")
input.audit: Signature check is dead code — both verified and unverified branches return the same User, so HMAC provides zero protection. Legacy X-User-Id header is trusted with no signature and the gateway does not strip it, giving a direct forgery channel. Auth failure returns None instead of raising, risking open-by-default downstream. No replay binding on the signed header and no network-level enforcement that the request actually came from the gateway.

content:
Think of X-Gateway-User-Id as answer to question "who are you?" written on outside of envelope. In our setup, only gateway should ever write that answer, because gateway does real authentication — checks session, checks token, knows caller — and then stamps envelope with user's id before forwarding to us.

Problem: HTTP headers aren't sealed. Anyone who can open TCP connection to our service can send request with any header they like, including one that claims "X-Gateway-User-Id: 1" or "X-Gateway-User-Id: ceo@company.com". Our service, sitting behind gateway, has no built-in way to tell "this header came from trusted gateway" apart from "this header came from attacker who bypassed gateway." Both look identical on wire.

That's why HMAC signature exists. Gateway signs id with shared secret; we verify signature before trusting id. No valid signature means header was not written by gateway, means we must reject. Current code takes id even when signature fails verification — so signature does nothing, and any attacker who reaches service directly becomes any user they name. That's full account takeover, no password needed, no session stolen, no bug in login flow. Just one forged header.

"Reaches service directly" sounds unlikely when you picture clean architecture diagram with one arrow from internet to gateway to service. Reality: services leak. Misconfigured load balancer, pod exposed on wrong port, SSRF vulnerability elsewhere that lets attacker make internal HTTP calls, compromised neighboring service doing lateral movement, developer port-forward left open, test environment sharing network with prod. Any of those turns "attacker can't reach us" into "attacker can reach us" — and at that moment, unverified identity headers become keys to every account.

Same logic applies to legacy X-User-Id header in diff. No signature at all, fully trusted, and gateway doesn't even strip it — so attacker doesn't need to bypass gateway, just sends X-User-Id alongside normal traffic and gateway forwards it untouched. Worse than Gateway-User-Id case because no defense was even attempted.

Rule of thumb: any header that carries identity or privilege must either be cryptographically bound to request (signed, with replay protection) and verified on every request, or must be impossible to reach from untrusted network (mTLS-pinned peer). Headers alone are suggestions. Signatures make them claims. Verified signatures make them facts.

To: Leadership
From: Engineering
Re: Auth middleware regression — summary and follow-up

What happened. During refactor of internal authentication layer, we introduced change that allowed any caller who could reach service directly to impersonate any user account. Root cause was two-part: first, new code accepted user identity from HTTP header that was supposed to be cryptographically signed by our gateway, but signature check was written in way that did nothing — code returned same result whether signature was valid or not. Second, older header carrying user identity was left in place with no signature check at all, and our gateway was not configured to strip it from incoming traffic. Either path, used alone, would have let attacker who bypassed gateway become any user on platform without password, session, or token.

Impact. No evidence of exploitation. Vulnerability was caught in code review before reaching production. Had it shipped, blast radius would have been every account, conditional on attacker obtaining network access to service behind gateway — realistic for sophisticated attacker via misconfigured infrastructure, SSRF in another service, or lateral movement from compromised internal system.

What we learned. Three points worth surfacing. First, "internal service behind gateway" is trust model that holds only as long as network boundary holds, and network boundaries fail more often than teams plan for — so identity claims must be verified at service itself, not assumed from position in network. Second, dead security checks are worse than no security checks, because they create illusion of protection while offering none; our review process did not have explicit step for "does this check actually reject bad input." Third, migrations that add new auth path while leaving old one running are high-risk moments — legacy header should have been removed, not left as fallback.

Going forward. Four changes. One, we are adding required security review for any change touching authentication or authorization code, with explicit sign-off separate from normal code review. Two, we are writing regression tests that send forged requests and assert rejection, so this specific class of bug cannot silently return in future. Three, we are tightening gateway configuration to strip all identity-related headers from inbound traffic, not only subset we previously tracked. Four, we are scheduling architecture review of service-to-service authentication to move from header-based trust toward mutual TLS, which binds identity to network connection rather than to easily-forged request metadata.

Timeline to completion: regression tests and gateway header stripping this week; required security review process within two weeks; mTLS architecture review scoped within month.

Happy to go deeper on any of above in person.
